top.value: The overall impact of the proposed action is the containment of the spread of COVID-19 in the targeted communities and the decrease and prevention of disease and malnutrition, including the decrease of loss of livelihoods and human assets. The outcomes we aim for is that target communities have taken the appropriate measures to stop COVID-19 transmission within the community and that the overall food and nutrition security of the target groups is not further impacted by the COVID-19 crisis. This will be achieved through access to humanitarian assistance across multiple response sectors, and protection services for human assets, social cohesion, rights and livelihoods enhancement.

A three-year Africa-wide program to mitigate humanitarian crises in seven countries of Sub-Saharan Africa – Burundi, Central African Republic, Ethiopia, Kenya, Niger, Somalia, South Sudan - is proposed here. The aim of the project is to ensure the basic provision of sufficient drinking water and access to safe sanitation and hygiene services as well as the provision of needs-based emergency aid to prevent or decrease morbidities, mortality and acute malnutrition especially for vulnerable people in acute humanitarian crises triggered by flight and displacement, droughts, floods and anticipated periods of hunger.

The project develops and establishes an Anticipatory Humanitarian Action Mechanism (Forecast-based Action/FbA) to address imminent risks of food insecurity in Madagascar, Zimbabwe and Kenya. The mechanism reduces expected impacts of drought hazards on vulnerable communities through the implementation of preventive Early Actions. Key elements are the scientific development of drought risk models, identification of trigger levels, risk and vulnerability analyses and the development of Early Action Protocols (EAPs).Scale-up and extension of the existing FbA mechanism in MadagascarReplication of the FbA mechanism in Zimbabwe and KenyaRespective EAPs are then linked to a Trigger-based Finance Facility to ensure proper financial coverage

To reduce effects of disasters on local communities in eight countries of intervention 40 local and national humanitarian organisations (five per country) and four international consortium partners will jointly develop and undertake capacity strengthening and exchange measures in order to reinforce their institutional and operational capacity for an effective and timely response to humanitarian crises and localised, principled humanitarian action. The ToGETHER programme promotes complementary partnerships and advocates for the local partners’ roles as leading local actors in humanitarian response, putting localisation - understood as transformation - into practice.

The project will build the economic, ecological and organisational resilience of 620,000 rural people in Burkina Faso and strengthen their ability to cope with the effects of increased rainfall variability and higher temperatures. This will be achieved by diversifying agricultural production and increasing incomes (through improved, sustainable access to drought-tolerant seeds, soil fertility improvement and enterprise development), together with strengthened government extension services to reduce crop losses and early-warning weather systems. The project will scale proven resilience-building approaches, embed climate adaptation approaches in local, regional and national plans, and generate and disseminate best practice guidance on climate adaptation approaches.

Since the Ebola crisis, everyone in Sierra Leone now recognises that better hygiene and a healthier environment are essential to prevent more epidemics. Unauthorised dumping sites and illegal waste disposal spread illnesses, so Welthungerhilfe is helping the government to develop an infrastructure for municipal waste collection. Sierra Leone's private sector is underdeveloped so it is expected to participate in providing public services and is also involved in this project. At the same time, jobs are being created for the country’s numerous unemployed young people. Awareness of waste reduction, separation and recycling is also being raised by various means such as through schools.

Impact: The basic needs regarding food, WASH and shelter are fulfilled and protection risks reduced for conflict affected IDPs and most vulnerable groups in Kachin State and Sagaing Region.Outcome: Immediate needs of IDPs and host communities with regards to food, WASH and shelter in 37 IDP camps and 10 host communities in in the target area are met.

In Liberia, the program targets 30 communities across 4 clans, in Suakoko district in Bong County that have lost forest and tree cover or are situated at the fringes of forests, where human activities threaten the forest’s sustainability. Bong county, with Suakoko as its largest commercial hub, recorded Liberia’s highest tree cover loss, totaling 409 kha between 2001 and 2023 . With an estimated population of 44,930 across three clans, Suakoko is a critical area for reforestation. To address urgent reforestation needs and prevent further deforestation, the program aims to reach 7,500 farming households, to conserve 50ha per community and to restore 150 hectares of degraded and forest-edge land. Engaging multiple communities and targeting both state and nonstate actors, marginalised groups, and 50% women, ensures that ecosystems benefit and sustainable livelihood opportunities reach those who traditionally have had limited access, thereby promoting fairness, social equity and sustainability.
The objective is to enable 3,000 smallholder farmers to have access to a new market as a result of EXTRA intervention.
Germany - Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development

1. Provide access to immediate life-saving WASH assistance (emergency response and early recovery) to flood-affected target communities in selected districts.

2. Provide access to comprehensive Mental Health and Psychosocial Support Services (MHPSS) and quality GBV care (in line with global IASC guidelines on MHPSS in humanitarian emergency settings)  to flood affected population in targeted districts.
Multi-purpose cash grants, cash for work/trainings and transport vouchers to access health services and shelter) is provided to the most vulnerable HHs affected by the floods (emergency response)
